entry.title: SAP Community Hobbies Series: From Crutches to Ironman 70.3 Finisher
entry.content:
My name is Vinit Kumar. I work as Development Architect in SAP Cloud Lifecycle Management (CLM) and I would like to share a bit about my hobby as part of the SAP Community Hobbies series. I’m an endurance athlete who discovered the incredible world of triathlon – a multi-sport discipline combining swimming, cycling, and running. What started as rehabilitation from a serious accident has evolved into my greatest passion. My hobby involves training for and competing in endurance events, with my crown achievement being the completion of Ironman 70.3 Goa.

 

I would love to share my journey of resilience and multi-sport endurance, culminating in becoming an Ironman 70.3 finisher in November 2022, which is one of my proudest moment.

 

The Journey

My journey began in the most unexpected way. In June 2014, I suffered a serious accident that left me with a fractured leg, metal plate, and 8 screws. For over a year, I couldn’t stand or walk for more than 30 minutes. It was my darkest period, both physically and mentally.

But sometimes adversity introduces us to our true selves. I decided to fight back with the philosophy that "pain will kill the pain." I started with jogging and running as therapy. The confidence booster for was to be able to complete a half marathon with implants in 2017.

The real spark came in May 2019 when I first heard about Ironman 70.3 Goa – this ultimate test of multi-sport endurance. The idea of earning the title "Ironman finisher" was intoxicating, even though I had no road cycling experience.

 

Why Ironman?

What I love most is the holistic nature of triathlon. It’s not just about physical strength – it builds mental resilience, discipline, and teaches you that limits are often self-imposed.

 

The diversity of training keeps things exciting: early morning swims, cycling through varied terrains, and the meditative rhythm of long runs.  But beyond that, it’s the personal transformation – going from someone who couldn’t walk 30 minutes to crossing an Ironman finish line after 7.5 hours.

The challenges

But also have to train the body and mind for endurance.

  • Physical setbacks: Starting with implants, then requiring another surgery to remove them, forcing me to restart from zero. I overcame this by viewing each setback as a new beginning rather than an end.
  • Skill gaps: Being new to road cycling was intimidating. I addressed this through consistent practice and learning from experienced cyclists.
  • Pandemic disruption: COVID-19 cancelled the 2020 and 2021 ironman Goa race, also made it difficult to maintain fitness and motivation. I stayed focused on my long-term goal and used the time to diversify my training.
  • Race day conditions: Ironman 70.3 Goa presented hot, humid conditions for my longest swim (1.9km in the sea) and longest bike ride (90km with 1100+ feet elevation gain). I overcame this by remembering: "pain is temporary, finishing is permanent."

The Learnings

  • Start where you are: I couldn’t walk 30 minutes – you don’t need to be fit to start getting fit.
  • Diversify your training: I added yoga, boxing, and strength training to build comprehensive fitness.

 

  • Embrace the "never give up" attitude: Discipline and persistence matter more than natural talent.
  • Focus on finishing, not winning: "Pain is temporary, finishing is permanent".
  • Build gradually: Respect the process and allow your body to adapt.
  • Find your community: Connect with fellow athletes to get invaluable guidance, support, and motivation.

 

Connection to Life @ SAP

Interestingly, triathlon and the SAP world share many parallels:

  1. Process Integration: Just like SAP integrates different business processes, triathlon integrates three distinct sports into one seamless experience.
  2. Performance Analytics: Both require constant monitoring and optimization – tracking training hours, performance metrics, and continuous improvement, similar to SAP’s focus on data-driven decisions.
  3. Systems Thinking: Success in both requires understanding how different components work together – in triathlon, how swim technique affects bike performance, and how bike effort impacts running, much like how SAP modules interconnect.
  4. Change Management: My journey from injury to Ironman mirrors organizational transformation – it requires planning, phased implementation, stakeholder buy-in, and persistence through challenges.
  5. Resilience and Continuity: Both demand building systems that can withstand disruption – whether it’s race day challenges or business continuity planning.

Remember, whether you’re implementing SAP solutions or training for an Ironman, the principle remains the same: "All it takes is all you got" If I can go from not being able to walk for 30 minutes to finishing an Ironman, imagine what you can achieve with dedication and the right mindset.

 

Thank You!

entry.title: SAP Community Voice: Joule-Powered Migration, Datasphere Insights and How to find your community!
entry.content:
In this edition of SAP Community Voice, we bring you a mix of innovation, technical deep dives, and inspiring community stories. From AI-driven custom code migration to SAP S/4HANA powered by Joule, to an in-depth exploration of SAP Datasphere's Object Store in Business Data Cloud, this week's highlights are packed with insights to help you stay ahead.

Beyond technology, we also celebrate the human side of our community, including an inspiring Ironman journey in our Hobbies Series, that proves resilience applies both in sports and in SAP projects. Plus, we have updates on the new CEI project for SAP Integration Suite, a reminder about the Ask Me Anything sessions with SAP Champions, and exciting event recaps and announcements from Southern California, Cyprus, and Philadelphia!

This edition is filled with practical insights, innovation, and global community energy. Let's dive in!

Featured Blog Posts

Custom code migration to SAP S/4HANA powered by Joule 

SAP expert Olga Dolinskaja, published a blog post about custom code migration to SAP S/4HANA powered by Joule for Developers. Considering that the conversion system to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition is often a complex and time-consuming process. Olga, talks about how IT project managers face challenges defining scope, estimating effort, and understanding legacy code that often lacks documentation, while developers manually analyze ATC findings and interpret simplification notes. To address these challenges and accelerate migration projects, she explains how AI-based capabilities within SAP Joule for Developers now provide intelligent support for both managers and developers. Integrated into the Custom Code Migration app on SAP BTP ABAP environment and ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse, these capabilities offer Code Explain for understanding legacy logic and business purpose, ATC Explain for clear root-cause analysis of S/4HANA findings and much more. By reducing manual effort and providing contextual insights directly within development tools, these innovations help shorten migration timelines, enabling teams to meet deadlines and budget constraints more confidently. 

Read Olga blog post here 

Deep Dive into SAP Datasphere Object Store of BDC 

The HANA Data Lake Files (HDLF) service in SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) Datasphere provides a cost-effective and scalable way to store and process large data volumes as Delta tables. SAP Community member, Sreekanth Surampally published a blog post to explain how inside BDC, SAP manages the Foundation Services (FOS) object store for standard SAP data products, while customers manage the Datasphere HDLF object store for their own SAP and non-SAP data. Through the document, he runs the details of building on hyperscale object storage and powered by Apache Spark, plus how HDLF enables large-scale replication and transformation flows, supporting medallion architecture (Bronze-Silver-Gold), and offering the "SQL on Files" feature to query Delta tables directly from HANA Cloud without loading data into memory. With images to illustrate the architecture and configurations, this is a must read if you are looking for depth and clarity about the topic. 

Read Sreekanth blog post here 

Joule A2A: Connect Code Based Agents into Joule 

SAP expert Felix Bartler, published valuable content about how to connect code based agents into Joule. Starting off considering that many organizations have already built custom AI agents using frameworks such as LangChain or LangGraph, his post demonstrates how such a custom-built agent can be integrated into the Joule ecosystem using the new pro-code extensibility features together with the A2A (Agent2Agent) protocol. A minimal ReAct-based currency conversion agent built in Python with LangGraph is exposed via an A2A server and deployed on SAP BTP. Using Joule capabilities defined in YAML (scenarios and functions), the agent is connected through an HTTP destination in SAP BTP and invoked via the new agent-request action type. Felix shows that the A2A protocol enables secure, structured communication between Joule and remote agents, while the Joule CLI supports compilation and deployment of the capability into a standalone or productive assistant.  

Read Felix blog post here 

UI for the BTP Application Vulnerability Report 

SAP Mentor Wouter Lemaire created a very useful blog post about the SAP BTP Application Vulnerability Report, which is a tool that scans your deployed applications for known vulnerabilities and exposes the findings through an API. To make this feature even better, he created an open-source UI I on top of the BTP Application Vulnerability Report API, to enable a better user experience to amplify and simplify the research. Running through the details of each step of his project, his solution enables sorting, filtering, free-text search, severity-based visual indicators, and Excel export of findings, making it easier to prioritize remediation and share reports with stakeholders. The project requires no database, as all data is fetched in real time from the API, and he also shared that the full source code is available on GitHub for contributions and enhancements. Try it out and give your feedback! 

Read Wouter blog post here 

Community News 

Hobbies Series: Ironman 

In this week's Hobbies Series, we are excited to share the inspiring story of Vinit Kumar, Development Architect in SAP Cloud Lifecycle Management (CLM), who discovered the world of triathlons in an unexpected way. In June 2014, Vinit suffered a serious accident, which left him with a fractured leg, a metal plate, and unable to walk for more than 30 minutes for over a year. Determined to recover, he began running as therapy and embracing the mindset that "pain will kill the pain." In 2017, he completed a half marathon despite still having implants, and in May 2019, he set his sights on Ironman 70.3 Goa, even without prior experience. Combining swimming, cycling, and running, the challenge became what he describes as a “teacher” about discipline, resilience, and the belief that limits are often self-imposed. Despite additional surgery and many problems on the preparation in Goa, Vinit kept repeating his mantra: "Pain is temporary, finishing is permanent." And in November 2022, after 7.5 hours across a 1.9 km sea swim, 90 km bike ride followed by a run, he proudly crossed the finish line. He also shared how triathlon mirrors life at SAP in many ways, like process integration and performance analytics to systems thinking, resilience, and change management. Proving that whether implementing SAP solutions or training for a race, the principle remains the same: all it takes is all you have got. 

Read Vinit blog post here 

Register to the new CEI project for SAP Integration Suite in 2026 

This year, the Global Customer Engagement Initiative (CEI) for SAP Integration Suite is back! SAP expert, Alex Bundschuh, shared on his latest blog post the details about the project on gathering early feedback on product improvements, planned enhancements, and new developments, ensuring they align with real business needs. Following last year's strong participation of over 180 customers and partners from more than 20 countries, for 2026 the discussions will cover key topics such as SAP PI/PO migration, AI and generative AI, developer experience, and much more. Registration is open from February 9 to March 13, 2026, with monthly feedback sessions starting in April and running through the end of the year. If you are interested in taking part, and help shaping the SAP Integration Suite product, make sure to register here (link leads to the customer influence page).  

Read Alex blog post here 

REMINDER: Ask Me Anything Sessions with SAP Champions  

Last edition we mentioned already about our SAP Champion Sebastiano Marchesini initiative Ask Me Anything, but we would like to remind everyone that this first upcoming session will be open for questions only until the end of this week (February 28). So, don´t miss out in the opportunity on participating on real discussions and getting to have your questions answered by an expert. Each month, a different SAP Champion with a specific area of expertise will answer both technical and non-technical questions, submitted openly or anonymously. For this first edition, selected questions will be answered in a recorded video published on the SAP Communities YouTube channel. For more details, make sure to read Sebastino full blog post bellow. 

Read Sebastiano blog post here 

Events 

SAP Southern California meetup recap 

This month was super exciting for the SAP Southern California local community, because they had their first meetup. Hosted by SAP Champion Vijayeta Sharma, the event brought together an inspiring group of professionals looking forward to connecting, exchanging ideas, and strengthening regional collaboration. The strong energy and enthusiasm set up a positive tone for future gatherings, with plans already underway for more meetups, knowledge-sharing sessions, and community-driven discussions. Participants are encouraged to share topic ideas and help shape events as the Southern California SAP network grows. So, if you are from the area or live close by, make sure to integrate into the SAP Community local group and participate in the next events.  

Read Vijayeta blog post here  

SAP Stammtisch in Cyprus recap 

In a recap shared by Vitaly Toraman, the third SAP Stammtisch in Cyprus once again brought together a vibrant group of SAP professionals, representing what Vitaly described as “nearly 100 years of combined SAP experience”. The session focused on S/4HANA transformation and highlighted five essential success factors: strong collaboration between business and IT, clear commitment and decision-making, true process simplification instead of lift-and-shift, structured change management, and early master data cleansing. The discussion underscored how valuable open exchange and local networking are in navigating complex transformation journeys, reinforcing the strength of the SAP Community in Cyprus (click here to join the group). So, if you are in the area, this is a perfect opportunity to connect with this incredible local community group. 

Read Vitaly blog post here 

SAP Inside Track Newtown Square, Philadelphia Summer 2026 

SAP Inside Track Philadelphia returns for its Summer 2026 edition! Planned for mid-June 2026 (9:00 AM - 4:00 PM EDT) at SAP America Headquarters in Newtown Square, PA (or a nearby location, as the organizing team are still planning the venue) this in-person event will once again bring together developers, partners, and SAP experts for a full day of knowledge sharing and networking. The 2026 agenda will focus on the next evolution of Business AI and Cloud ERP, covering topics such as Agentic AI, Joule integrations across SAP solutions, Clean Core strategies with RISE and GROW, and hands-on demos with SAP S/4HANA and Analytics. The organizers are currently building the agenda and invite community members interested in speaking to get involved. If you are in the area or would like to participate, the SAP community in Newtown Square (click here to join the group) is welcoming you. Mark your calendar and stay tuned for more details. 

Read Johna blog post here

entry.title: Achieve Event Driven Architecture and FIFO Approach with Google PubSub Adapter in Cloud Integration
entry.content:
With the growing popularity of Event Drive Architecture (EDA), message based adapters are a popular choice. Google PubSub Adapter for SAP Integration Suite is one such adapter. Understanding the architecture of the Google PubSub Subscription is a complex undertaking, therefore this blog attempts to simplify the same by creating a simple Integration Flow (iflow) to achieve Event Driven Architecture with FIFO approach.

Additionally majority of EDA based use cases revolve around the First In First Out (FIFO) Approach. Take E-Commerce for example. One would want that unless an order has been successfully processed there should not be any inventory change or the status of an order should not change to Shipped unless the payment has been completed. Such a crucial mechanism needs to be handled without any chance of error. Using Google PubSub with the necessary configurations, we can create such a scenario with ease.

Google PubSub Concepts

Before getting into the Integration flow, let's talk a little about the architecture of Google PubSub(GPS) itself.

GPS has three main components, a topic, the subscription attached to the topic, and the message that flows from topic to subscription. When a message is published to a topic, a messageId and publishTime is attached to it. Uniqueness of a message is decided by the assigned messageId.

Configuring Google PubSub Topic

Create a new topic and give it a unique ID. You could choose to have a default subscription attached to it. The ID of the default subscription would be decided by GPS itself (usually appending "-sub" after the Topic ID). Keep the rest of the configurations unchecked as they are not relevant to our use case.

Configuring Google PubSub Subscription

You can create a new subscription or work with the default subscription attached to the topic.

If you create a new subscription ensure that it is of Pull delivery type. This ensure subscribers to request for message using the Google PubSub Sender Adapter.

A Pull delivery type subscription has the following configurations.

The important configurations to create an FIFO Event Driven architecture are:

  • Exactly once delivery: Enable to ensure messages are not resent before the Acknowledgement Deadline expires.
  • Message ordering: Enable to ensure that messages with the same ordering key are received by the subscription in the order they were published.
  • Acknowledgement deadline (optional): Decides in how much time an acknowledgement is expected. If no acknowledgement is received within the expiration of this deadline, GPS topic is free to send the message to any of its subscriptions. You must set an acknowledgement deadline greater than the maximum integration flow processing time. This field technically does not affect the FIFO approach but setting a higher value is advantageous in terms of redelivery attempts.

Configuring Google PubSub Sender (REST)

Below is a basic iflow to read messages from a GPS Subscription. An exception subprocess block has been added to handle any error that occurs.

Ensure that the exception sub process ends with an Error End Event.

This is extremely important. The behavior of the Integration Suite and the adapter itself depends highly on these events. Error End Event tells the adapter that the business process flow encountered an error and a negative Message Processing Log (MPL) is created. This negative MPL is dealt with differently as compared to a positive MPL.

After configuring the Connection tab of the GPS Sender (for more info, see SAP Cloud Integration – Getting started with Google Pub/Sub Adapter), switch to Processing tab.

Project ID : ID assigned to your Google Cloud Console project.

Subscription ID: ID of the subscription from which messages will be read.

Max Messages to Read: Maximum number of messages to be read in a single poll. 

More details for these values which can be found here SAP Help.

Select Post-Processing as Acknowledge after Successful Processing; this ensures that the message is not acknowledged by the adapter, if the iflow processing ends with an error i.e. a negative MPL was created. Remember our previous explanation concerning Error End Event.

Following these recommendations and values will ensure an iflow with FIFO approach is successfully designed.

Sample Use Case 1 : Messages with same Ordering Key

The flow is as follows:

  1. Messages M1 and M2 are published to a GPS Topic with the same Ordering key. You can achieve this in a different iflow using the Google PubSub Receiver Adapter or via an external publisher system.
  2. The first message M1 is read by the GPS Sender attached to our iflow. Note that since M1 and M2 were part of the same ordering key and the subscription had Ordering Enabled, GPS does not allow M2 to be even sent before M1 is out of the Topic.
  3. The GPS Sender sends the message M1 to the iflow for processing.
  4. Lets say an error occurs while processing this message M1. The Exception Sub-Process is invoked, and after the necessary steps (as per your business logic; e.g.. create a JIRA ticket), iflow ends with an Error End Event.
  5. A negative MPL is created for the processing of M1. GPS Sender receives this negative MPL back and does not send an acknowledgement to the GPS Subscription.
  6. GPS Subscription does not receive an Acknowledgement for M1 within the set acknowledgement deadline. M1 remains in the topic. 
  7. Google PubSub Sender will not receive the second message M2 until M1 is removed from its topic either by acknowledging it or by moving it to a Dead Letter Queue using a different configuration or by manually removing it.

Sample Use Case 2: Messages with different Ordering Keys

Let us see a similar thing with two messages of same ordering key and another message with a different ordering key.

Messages M1 and M2 published with same ordering key 1

      

Message M3 with a different ordering key 3 published.

Iflow receives M1 and M3 repeatedly until they are successfully processed by the iflow. M2 was not received as its predecessor M1 was not processed completely and was not removed from the topic.

  

 

Enabled Ordering Key in the Subscription ensures that M1 is processed before M2 as they have the same ordering key and M1 was published before M2.

Enabled Exactly Once Delivery ensure M1 is not resent until Acknowledgement Deadline expiration occurs which in this case was 120 seconds.

Conclusion

By leveraging the Google PubSub Adapter in SAP Integration Suite we are able to achieve strict message ordering which is a must if you are focusing on the integrity of an Event Driven Business Model. You can avoid a scenario where "Update" event comes before "Create" event in a database call.

In short Event Driven Architecture means your system stays updated in real-time, FIFO ensures those updates happen in the correct sequence, and Google PubSub Adapter ensures Event Driven Architecture is implemented with FIFO.

Happy Integrating!

entry.title: Joule Capabilities for Developers ABAP
entry.content:
Introduction :

Joule is SAP’s AI-powered copilot designed to assist developers and business users across SAP applications. For developers, Joule offers:

  • Context-aware code suggestions.
  • Real-time explanations of ABAP objects.
  • Automation of repetitive tasks.
  • Support for full-stack ABAP Cloud development.

Joule Capabilities for Developers

Feature

Description

Code Prediction

Suggests next lines of ABAP code contextually

Object Explanation

Provides real-time insights into ABAP objects

Scenario Generation

Creates full-stack development templates

Legacy Code Support

Helps interpret and refactor old codebases

Productivity Boost

Reduces development and testing time by ~20–25%

Prerequisites

Before starting the setup, ensure the following components are available:

Component

Purpose

SAP BTP Subaccount

Hosting and managing services

Cloud Foundry Environment

Runtime for deploying applications

SAP Cloud Identity Services

Authentication and SSO

SAP Build Work Zone (Standard Edition)

UI integration and launchpad

SAP Business Application Studio (BAS)

Development environment

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

ERP system with Joule capabilities

 License:

To leverage Joule’s capabilities, you need to purchase an additional license:

  1. Customers: 8019124 - Joule for developers limited promotion, ABAP AI
  2. Partners (TDD Subscription): 8019541 - Cloud TDD, Joule for developers ABAP AI

For more details, please check SAP Note 3571857

For SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition the subscription needs to be requested via SAP for Me

Product availability:

  1. SAP BTP ABAP Environment as of release 2502
  2. SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition as of release 2502
  3. SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition as of release 2025.

Setup:

Embedded Joule: It is used by Business users. Below URL for joule setup.

Joule in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - Setup ... - SAP Community

Joule for Developers

It is used by Developers in ADT (Eclipse).

Set up in  Public cloud environment : https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/joule-for-developers-with-sap-s-4hana-public-cloud-edition-setup-guide/ba-p/14209989

Set up in BTP environment:

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/joule-for-developers-with-sap-btp-abap-environment-setup-guide/ba-p/14207462

Joule for Developers ABAP : Helps developers in below use cases

  • Predictive Code Completion: Suggests next lines of code based on context.
  • Code Explanation: Understand legacy or complex ABAP and CDS code
  •  OData consumption helpers: Generate client proxy calls
  •  RAP business objects generation
  •  Automated unit test generation for ABAP

Predictive code completion

In RAP development, the system interprets the developer’s intent and recommends appropriate code, as demonstrated in the screenshot below.

Alternatively, the system can generate code from natural‑language prompts. To do this, position the cursor inside the method and choose Quick Help.

Click Run button after entering the prompt. Look at code generated in below screen shot.

Code explanation:

To understand the functionality of any class or CDS view, open the class and type “/”, then select the Explain option as mentioned below screen shot.

Loot at the generated summary for selected class.

OData consumption:

The Service Consumption Model in ABAP (created in ABAP Development Tools, ADT/Eclipse) generates a typed OData client proxy from an OData metadata (EDMX/service URL). You then use that proxy to call an external OData service in a safe, OO way—no manual HTTP/JSON handling needed.

Use a Service Consumption Model if you need to consume (call) an external OData service from ABAP—for example:

  • Reading products/orders from a partner system (OData V2 or V4).
  • Calling SAP S/4HANA Cloud APIs (which are exposed as OData).
  • Integrating public demo services (e.g., Northwind) for POCs.

Choose mode as OData and click on finish.

Enter the name of the class and upload the metadata file.

Loot at generated one and sample code for entity set access.

Create one global class to consume the model

Right click on model and choose consume option from Joule.

Copy and paste source code from Joule.

RAP business objects generation:

In the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP), a Business Object (BO) represents the data model, behaviour, and business logic of an application. A RAP BO is generated using a set of design‑time artifacts.

A complete RAP BO typically includes:

  1. Data Model (via CDS Views)
  2. Behaviour Definition (BDEF)
  3. Behaviour Implementation (ABAP classes)
  4. Service Definition
  5. Service Binding

Right click on the package and choose Generate ABAP Repository Objects as mentioned below screen shot.

Choose OData UI Service from Scratch

Look at Joule icon in the left side of the screen.

Enter the prompt as per our requirement.

Click on Accept

Look at generated table and adjust or add any fields if required.

Look at generated artifacts.

Click on Next button and Observe the system generated Artifacts.

Activate the Service and test the service.

Automated unit test generation for ABAP:

Joule can generate ABAP Unit tests for public, protected, and private methods of global classes, and for public methods of local classes in global class pools.

CDS test classes (CDS unit tests) in addition to ABAP class tests.

Right click on class and choose Joule with Unit option i.e. / Unit.

Select Generate test for selected methods.

Tick check boxes methods which are required.

Look at generated test class by the Joule.

 

For testing, Right click on class and choose ABAP Unit test.

Look at system generate results as mentioned in screen.

entry.title: Społeczności z powiatu SAPowiskiego spotyka poraz pierwszy w 2026 roku!
entry.content:
Nowe osoby w grupie

Dołączyło do nas kilka nowych osób, które wniosły świeżą perspektywę i sporo ciekawych pytań dotyczących rozwiązań AI oraz ich zastosowania w realnych projektach. Cieszymy się, że jesteście z nami. Liczymy, że poczujecie się tu jak w transakcji, która wreszcie działa – czyli tak, jakby ktoś włączył debuger i wyłączył wszystkie niepotrzebne user-exit’y.

Nowe podejście do rzeczywistości AI

Dyskutowaliśmy o tym, jak dynamicznie zmienia się rynek i w jaki sposób sztuczna inteligencja wchodzi do codziennych procesów: od automatyzacji, przez analizę danych, po nowe modele pracy konsultantów i deweloperów.

Wspólny wniosek był prosty: AI nie jest już dodatkiem do projektów, ale jednym z filarów ich realizacji. To wymusza nowe podejście do nauki, planowania i samego patrzenia na systemy SAP.

Co nowego w świecie SAP

Omówiliśmy m.in.:

  • nowe kursy i aktualizacje ścieżek certyfikacyjnych,

  • materiały przygotowujące do egzaminów SAP z elementami AI,

  • nowości w narzędziach wspierających pracę konsultantów, w tym rozwijane funkcje oparte na modelach generatywnych.

SAP coraz mocniej akcentuje połączenie klasycznego know-how procesowego z kompetencjami AI. To będzie jeden z kluczowych kierunków rozwoju w 2026 roku.

Plany na następne miesiące

Na kolejnym spotkaniu wracamy z jeszcze większą porcją wiedzy i dyskusji. Zapowiadają się sesje techniczne, prezentacje wdrożeniowe oraz przegląd nowinek, które już teraz kształtują przyszłość rynku.

Dziękujemy wszystkim za udział i zaangażowanie. Ten rok dopiero się zaczyna, a my już widzimy, że będzie bardzo ciekawy. Jeśli to dopiero rozgrzewka, to kolejne spotkania zapowiadają się naprawdę interesująco. 

Link do następnego spotkania: https://community.sap.com/t5/katowice-events/katowice-sap-stammtisch-15-04-2026/ev-p/14328630

Pozdrawiamy serdecznie!

SAP Cpmmunity Katowice

entry.title: SAP Community CPH Event Plan - 2026
entry.content:
SAP Community Copenhagen – Event Plan for 2026

We are excited to share our SAP Community plan for 2026!

This year we are aiming at organizing at least four events – one per quarter – combining professional inspiration with cozy community social time.

In Q1, before Easter, we will do a social meet-up. This will be a great opportunity to reconnect, meet new members, and strengthen our community. The date will be the 26th of March and the event for sign up will be shared shortly.

In Q2, at the end of May after the SAPPHIRE conference in Madrid (19-21 May), we will host an event where we would like to invite 1–2 speakers from Denmark or abroad to share key announcements and highlights from SAPPHIRE. We are aiming at the 28th of May for this event.

After the summer holidays, in Q3, we will meet again for a social event while the weather is still good – a perfect setting for socializing and sharing our holiday pictures.

Finally, after SAP TechEd in the fourth quarter, we are planning a half-day or full-day event with inspirational talks from speakers from Denmark and abroad, focusing on the latest trends and insights. 

The dates for the second half of 2026 are still TBD. 

On behalf of the SAP Community CPH organizers, we are really looking forward to an inspiring year together and to growing our SAP Community even stronger in 2026!

Juan, Prakash, Pallavi, Christian, Asier

entry.title: SAP Stammtisch Plus - Pune (March 2026)
entry.content:
Greetings SAP Community in Pune! We have some exciting news to share with you!


SAP Community events are back in Pune!

We are thrilled to announce that we’re launching a series of quarterly SAP Stammtisch Plus gatherings, beginning with our first meetup on March 14th, 2026.

This event is an excellent opportunity for SAP professionals, enthusiasts, and anyone interested in learning more about SAP technologies to come together, learn, network with like-minded individuals, and have fun.

Are you wondering what an SAP Stammtisch Plus event is?

SAP Stammtisch Plus is a local grassroots community event where SAP Community members come together to share knowledge about SAP-related topics. The event's primary focus is networking & discussions, with 2 or 3 sessions from community members.

Learn more about it at: SAP Stammtisch - what is it about?

What is in it for you?

Let’s cut to the chase, what will you get by attending or contributing to this event?

1. Networking – You get to meet like-minded tech folks and have more space for networking.
2. Learning – You will learn about the latest SAP technologies from experts.

Theme
